feed_card.one_line: An unusually high or low result is usually followed by a more ordinary one, because luck rarely repeats.
teasers: Your best month ever is a bad sign for the next one.
The cure you swear by may just be good timing.
Why does punishment seem to work and praise seem to backfire?

sections.content: A sales rep closes a record number of deals in March, the best month anyone on the team has seen. Management promotes her and raises her target. April comes in lower. The natural reading is that success went to her head, or the new pressure hurt. The duller reading is closer to the truth. March combined real ability with a run of warm leads, a competitor who stumbled, and a few clients who happened to be ready to buy that week. The ability carried into April. The lucky run did not. Strip out the luck and what remains is closer to ordinary, which is roughly where April landed. A March that good was never going to be her normal.

content.image_caption: Each dot is one thing measured twice. The solid line runs flatter than the dashed line that marks an equal second score, so after a high first score the second tends to come down toward the average, and after a low first score it tends to come up.

content.title: Every result mixes signal and luck
content.body: Any measured performance has two parts. One is stable: real skill, true quality, the underlying rate. The other is transient, drawn from luck, timing, mood, measurement error, and the conditions of that one day. A good day at work is part real ability and part how the day happened to break.

content.title: Extremes need both parts aligned
content.body: A record high happens when the stable part is strong and the luck runs favorable at the same moment. Both pushing the same way at once is uncommon, which is why records are rare. The mirror image holds for a record low, where a weak underlying rate meets a bad draw of luck.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='60 72 310 203' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='70' y1='240' x2='360' y2='240' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'/><rect x='110' y='150' width='70' height='90' rx='6' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='110' y='82' width='70' height='68' rx='6' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.18' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><rect x='250' y='150' width='70' height='90' rx='6' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='250' y='128' width='70' height='22' rx='6' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.18' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M188 92 Q225 100 248 124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-dasharray='4 4' opacity='0.5'/><path d='M248 124 l1 -9 m-1 9 l-9 -1' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.5'/><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='145' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>skill</text><text x='285' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>skill</text><text x='145' y='120' fill='var(--accent)' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>luck</text><text x='285' y='120' fill='var(--accent)' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>luck</text><text x='145' y='262' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75'>Record day</text><text x='285' y='262' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75'>Next day</text></g></svg>

content.title: Luck resets on the next measurement
content.body: Measure the same thing again and the stable part is still there. The favorable luck is gone. A fresh draw of luck takes its place, and on average that draw is ordinary.

content.title: The result drifts back toward average
content.body: With only the stable part and an ordinary draw of luck, the second measurement settles closer to the mean than the first. The more the first result owed to luck, the larger the drop. Nothing has to act on the performer for this to happen. The second reading is the same skill, now showing up on a more ordinary day.

content.title: Weak correlation means strong regression
content.body: How far a result regresses depends on how closely two measurements of the same thing agree. When they agree tightly, the second barely moves. Measure height twice with a good tape and the readings nearly match. When they agree only loosely, as with a sales rep's month, the second falls most of the way back to the average.

In standardized terms, the effect has an exact size. Each measurement can be written as the number of standard deviations it sits from the average. The best prediction for the second standardized score is the first standardized score multiplied by the correlation between the two measurements. That correlation runs from -1 to 1. Since anything short of a perfect 1 shrinks the score when you multiply, the predicted second score keeps the same sign but lands closer to zero, the mean. A correlation of 0.5 means a first score two standard deviations above average predicts a second score only one standard deviation above average.

notation_legend.meaning: the first measurement, in standard deviations from the mean
the predicted second measurement, in standard deviations from the mean
the correlation between the two measurements, which runs from -1 to 1

content.title: Rookie award and the year after
content.body: An athlete wins the season's top rookie award, then disappoints in the second season, and commentators reach for reasons: complacency, pressure, opponents catching on. The rookie award goes to whoever combined real talent with a season where the breaks fell their way. The talent carries over to the next season. The lucky breaks that came with the award were never going to repeat at the same rate. A second season nearer the player's true level reads as a decline only against an inflated baseline. The same logic sits behind the idea that athletes fade after appearing on a famous sports magazine's cover.

content.title: Speed cameras after a crash spike
content.body: Authorities install speed cameras where serious crashes recently spiked. Crashes then fall, and the camera is declared a success. Some of that fall is real deterrence. A large part is regression: a site gets chosen precisely because it had an unusually bad year, and unusually bad years are followed by ordinary ones whether or not a camera arrives. Road safety studies that ignore this overstate the camera's benefit.

content.title: The doctor visit at the worst moment
content.body: People book a doctor when symptoms are at their worst, because that is what drives them to call. Many conditions then ease regardless of what the doctor does, since the visit was timed to a low point that was always going to lift. The patient credits the new medicine and the doctor credits the treatment.

content.title: Flight instructors
content.body: Air force flight instructors noticed that praising a cadet after an excellent maneuver was followed by a worse one, while shouting at a cadet after a poor maneuver was followed by improvement. They concluded that criticism works and praise backfires. The pattern was regression. An excellent maneuver is a high outlier whose next attempt lands closer to average, and a poor one is a low outlier whose next attempt lands better. The instructors were rewarding and punishing chance.

Reach for regression to the mean whenever you are about to draw a lesson from a before-and-after comparison. Ask first why this case landed in front of you. Often you picked it because it was a high or a low point: a record quarter, a worst-ever month, a patient at their sickest. When that is the reason, expect the next measurement to move toward the average on its own, before you credit anything you did. The harder you selected on the extreme, the more free movement you should expect. To separate a real effect from this drift, you need a comparison: a group that was equally extreme and left untreated. If your treated group improves no more than that control, the change was regression. If it beats the control, you have found something real. When no control exists, at least ask whether the size of the change is larger than chance alone would produce.
checklist: Was this case chosen because it was a high or a low point?
Does the measure carry luck, timing, mood, or noise?
Is there a control that was equally extreme but left alone?
Did the change beat what regression alone would produce?
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='10 108 381 86' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='20' y='118' width='105' height='66' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='148' y='118' width='105' height='66' rx='12' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><rect x='276' y='118' width='105' height='66' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M125 151 h23'/><path d='M148 151 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/><path d='M253 151 h23'/><path d='M276 151 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='72' y='146' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>Picked for</text><text x='72' y='164' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>an extreme?</text><text x='200' y='146' fill='var(--accent)' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>Expect drift</text><text x='200' y='164' fill='var(--accent)' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>to the mean</text><text x='328' y='146' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>Beat a fair</text><text x='328' y='164' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>control?</text></g></svg>

sections.content: Regression to the mean is a property of selection plus noise, not a force, and treating it as a force creates its own errors. The most common is over-diagnosis: waving away a genuine effect as “just regression” when a proper control shows the treated group improved more than chance would deliver. A second error is to forget which average a result returns to. A result settles toward the mean of its own group, so a consistently elite performer regresses toward an elite average, which sits well above the population's middle. Regression also says nothing certain about a single next case. It describes a tendency across many cases, and any one performer can still post a second extreme. In 1933 Horace Secrist filled a book with charts of firms drifting toward “mediocrity” and concluded that competition erodes excellence. The statistician Harold Hotelling reviewed it and showed the drift was nothing but regression to the mean.

content.body: When an extreme result settles back toward normal, we are quick to credit a cause, a manager who turned things around or a remedy that worked. Often the only thing at work is arithmetic.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='56 44 260 170' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='70' y1='180' x2='306' y2='180' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-dasharray='5 5' opacity='0.4'/><text x='70' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.6'>average</text><circle cx='250' cy='80' r='6' fill='var(--accent)'/><path d='M250 90 Q300 135 256 172' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M256 172 l9 -3 m-9 3 l3 -9' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='250' y='64' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>standout</text></svg>

content.question: A sales manager praises reps after their best month and criticizes them after their worst. Over time she sees that criticized reps usually improve while praised reps usually slip, and concludes that criticism is the more effective tool. What is she most likely missing?
options: Her read holds up, since the numbers consistently show criticism beating praise
A best or worst month is mostly luck sitting on steady skill, so each rep's next month drifts back toward their own normal whatever she says
Praise breeds complacency while criticism sharpens focus, and that is what moves the numbers
The reps she criticized happened to draw easier targets the following month
content.explanation: Best and worst months sit at the extremes, where luck runs strongest, so the month after tends back toward each rep's usual level on its own. The feedback collects the credit, though the same swing would appear if she said nothing.

content.question: A supplement is advertised with glowing testimonials from people who started taking it when they felt at their absolute worst. Why is this evidence weak?
options: People who feel their worst tend to improve afterward anyway, with or without the supplement
Testimonials are always fabricated
The sample is too small to matter
The improvement is real, so the supplement must deserve some of the credit
content.explanation: Starting “at their worst” selects a low point, and low points are followed by improvement on their own. The testimonials may be honest and still reveal nothing about the supplement, because regression predicts the same recovery without it.

content.question: Which case shows the LEAST regression to the mean between two measurements?
options: A basketball player's points across two different games
A restaurant's busiest single night and the night after
A stock's best trading day and the following day
A person's height measured twice with a good tape measure
content.explanation: Height measured carefully is almost pure signal, so the two measurements correlate near 1 and barely regress. The others all carry heavy day-to-day luck, which is exactly what produces large regression.

content.question: An investor picks the single best-performing fund of the past year and expects it to lead again next year. What does regression to the mean predict?
options: The fund will almost certainly lead again
The fund will likely perform closer to the average next year
The fund will become the worst performer
The fund's strategy is proven, so its edge will persist
content.explanation: Topping the table in one year usually combines real skill with a favorable run. The skill may persist, but the lucky run is unlikely to repeat, so the expected next-year result sits closer to the pack, not at the top and not at the bottom.

content.question: Why does a clinical trial need a control group even when patients on the new drug get better?
options: Because patients enrolled at their sickest would tend to improve anyway, and the control measures that baseline drift
Because without a control, every gain in the treated group has to be chalked up to regression rather than to the drug
Because improvement and the drug only move together, and a correlation like that can never establish cause
Because drugs only work in comparison to other drugs
content.explanation: Patients often enter a trial at a low point, so some recovery is regression, not the drug. A control group equally sick at the start shows how much improvement happens without treatment, so any extra gain in the treated group can be credited to the drug.

content.question: A chess grandmaster plays her best tournament ever, far above her usual elite level. What does regression to the mean predict for her next tournament?
options: Around the average of all tournament players
Her worst tournament, to balance out the best one
Exactly the same, since her skill has not changed
Strong but closer to her own high average, a step down from her personal best
content.explanation: Regression pulls a result toward the mean of its own group, which for her is an elite average. Her skill is real and stays high, so her next result settles near that personal average, well above ordinary players and a step below her best.

content.question: A manager dismisses a training program's measured gains by saying “that is just regression to the mean.” When is the manager wrong?
options: Whenever a before-and-after gain appears, regression fully explains it
The manager is never wrong, since all gains are regression
When a matched group that was equally weak and skipped the training improved much less than the trained group
When the improvement was large, since a big gain cannot be only regression
content.explanation: Regression is the right suspicion, not the final verdict. If a control group that started equally weak improved far less, the trained group's extra gain is a real effect that regression cannot account for.

content.body: Francis Galton named the effect in 1886, in a paper titled *Regression towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature*. He had gathered the heights of more than nine hundred adult children alongside their parents and noticed a pattern that puzzled him: tall parents had children taller than average, but shorter than the parents themselves, and short parents had children below average yet taller than themselves. Each generation leaned back toward the population's middle. Galton first called this “reversion,” then settled on “regression,” and the word later attached itself to the whole statistical method of fitting a line through data. The mathematics that explains it was sharpened by his protégé Karl Pearson into the correlation coefficient. Galton also coined the word “eugenics” and pursued the heredity studies in its service, a use of the same data that the science has long since rejected.

key_thinkers.name: Francis Galton
key_thinkers.role: Named and first described the effect
key_thinkers.one_line: Victorian polymath who measured heredity, coined the term “regression,” and also founded eugenics.

key_thinkers.name: Karl Pearson
key_thinkers.one_line: Galton's protégé, who turned the idea into the correlation coefficient and much of modern statistics.

content.distinction: The gambler's fallacy expects independent events to self-correct, as if a coin that landed heads five times now “owes” tails. Regression involves no such force and no memory between events. It describes how a selected extreme, made partly of luck, tends to look milder on remeasurement.

Financial mean reversion is an economic claim that prices tend back toward a fundamental value, a statement about a real process that may or may not hold. Statistical regression to the mean is a property of noisy measurement and selection, and it appears even when nothing in the world pulls anything back.

content.label: Galton F. Regression towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature. Journal of the Anthropological Institute 1886;15:246-263
Barnett AG, van der Pols JC, Dobson AJ. Regression to the mean: what it is and how to deal with it. International Journal of Epidemiology 2005;34(1):215-220
Kahneman D. Thinking, Fast and Slow. Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011 (Chapter 17, Regression to the Mean)
Stigler SM. The History of Statistics in 1933. Statistical Science 1996;11(3) (covers Hotelling's refutation of Secrist's regression fallacy)
